source.title: When fear is near: threat imminence elicits prefrontal-periaqueductal gray shifts in humans

confidence_rationale: High source quality (Science, human fMRI) and strong mapping fidelity: vmPFC-to-PAG shift is a direct neural instantiation of ARC-024's gradient structure. Harm signal magnitude increases continuously as threat proximity increases, before contact occurs. Modest transfer risk because the virtual-threat paradigm may not capture all naturalistic gradient properties.
failure_signatures: Small sample (n=14) limits generalisability of the vmPFC-PAG gradient finding.
Virtual predator paradigm with controlled shock delivery may produce cleaner gradients than real-world harm sources, potentially overestimating gradient smoothness.
Shock magnitude was known to participants; real-world harm is uncertain, which could alter the shape of the gradient.

mapping.source_claim_statement: As a virtual predator approaches, human fMRI activity shifts continuously from ventromedial prefrontal cortex (distal threat) to periaqueductal gray (proximal/imminent threat), with PAG activity correlating with dread and escape confidence.
mapping.ree_translation: The vmPFC-to-PAG shift is the neural correlate of ARC-024's harm gradient: the brain encodes harm signal intensity proportional to proximity to the harm source before contact occurs. The world-latent encoding of harm (hazard_field in CausalGridWorldV2) recapitulates this gradient structure computationally.
mapping.mapping_caveat: The study measures spatial imminence (distance between agent and predator) rather than the world-latent representation of harm. The inference from neural gradient to latent-space gradient requires an additional step.
mapping.source_context: Human fMRI, n=14 healthy participants, virtual maze, virtual predator, electrical shock upon capture

confidence_components.notes: Science journal, human data, well-replicated finding. Transfer risk is low for the gradient-before-contact point but moderate for the latent-space interpretation.
